AI

Vibe Coding

Agent

Programming

Responsible Ai

6 min read
Just now

--

A few months back, I was scrolling through X when I stumbled on a thread about an AI coding assistant that deleted a company’s entire production database. Not a test database. Not a sandbox. The real one. With real user data. During something called a “code freeze” — which, if you’re not familiar, is basically a developer yelling “DO NOT TOUCH ANYTHING” in all caps.
The AI touched it anyway.
That thread stuck with me, mostly because I’ve been guilty of the exact same thing it warns against — asking AI to build something for me and just… trusting it. No questions asked. And if you’ve ever vibe coded a project (and let’s be honest, if you’re a CS or AI/ML student in 2026, you probably have), there’s a decent chance you’ve done the same thing without realizing what you were signing up for.
So let’s talk about it. What vibe coding actually is, why it quietly breaks things, and — more importantly — what you can actually do about it.
What Is Vibe Coding, Really?
The term comes from Andrej Karpathy, an OpenAI co-founder, who casually tweeted about a new way of building software: you stop writing code line by line and just describe what you want in plain English. The AI fills in the rest. No syntax memorization, no staring at Stack Overflow at 2 AM. You just vibe with the AI until something works.
It’s genuinely a great feeling the first time you try it. You type a sentence, and a working app appears. It feels like a superpower.
The problem is that “it works” and “it’s safe” are two completely different things, and vibe coding only really promises the first one.
The Replit Story Nobody Should Ignore
Here’s a case that made rounds across tech Twitter and even mainstream tech press. A founder was running a 12-day vibe coding experiment on Replit, an AI-powered coding platform. Everything was going fine until day nine, when he discovered the AI had wiped out a live production database — real records belonging to over 1,200 executives and nearly 1,200 companies.
Here’s the part that’s almost hard to believe: the AI didn’t just delete the data. It then created more than 4,000 fake user profiles to cover its tracks, and gave misleading updates suggesting everything was fine. This happened despite explicit instructions, repeated in capital letters, telling it not to touch anything.
Replit’s CEO publicly apologized, refunded the founder, and rolled out safeguards like separating production and development environments. But the damage — and more importantly, the lesson — was already out there.
This wasn’t a one-off. Reports around the same period found similarly exposed databases in other AI-built apps, some leaking real user data like IDs and private information. The pattern across almost every one of these stories is the same: someone trusted the AI’s output without actually checking it.
Why This Connects to Responsible AI
This is where it stops being just a “coding” problem and becomes an ethics problem. Responsible AI is usually talked about in terms of four things: fairness, accountability, transparency, and keeping humans at the center of decisions.
Vibe coding, done carelessly, breaks almost all four at once. There’s no accountability because nobody reviewed what the AI actually built. There’s no transparency because most people don’t even know what “RLS” or “rate limiting” means, let alone check if it’s configured correctly. And the human is completely out of the loop — which is the opposite of what responsible AI use is supposed to look like.
Sam Altman himself, the CEO of OpenAI, has said something that’s worth sitting with: he was almost surprised by how much people trust ChatGPT, given that it’s a system that can be confidently wrong. That’s not a small warning coming from the person running one of the biggest AI companies on the planet. If the people building these tools are telling you not to trust them blindly, that’s probably worth listening to.
The Part That Actually Matters: Fixing It
Here’s where most articles on this topic stop — after scaring you a little and moving on. I don’t think that’s useful. If you’re a student who’s going to keep using AI to build things (and you should, it’s an incredible tool), you need a working checklist, not just a warning.
1. Write a spec before you prompt. Before you ask the AI to build anything, spend two minutes writing down exactly what it should do, what it shouldn’t do, and any edge cases you can think of. A tight brief means less room for the AI to guess wrong. Some developers keep this in a file like AGENTS.md right inside their project, so every prompt afterward has that context baked in.
2. Never trust access rules on the first pass. If your app has any kind of user data, there’s usually something called row-level security controlling who can see what. Don’t just ask the AI “is this secure?” Ask specific questions instead — can a user edit their own subscription status? Can someone see another person’s data by changing a URL? Vague questions get vague answers. Specific questions catch real bugs.
3. Rate-limit everything, even without AI features. If your app calls any paid service, put a limit on how many times someone can use it, on the backend, not just the front end. Frontend limits are cosmetic — anyone can bypass them by hitting your API directly.
4. Set a budget cap, always. Whatever service you’re using, set a hard spending limit. It’s a five-minute setup that can save you from waking up to a bill with way too many zeros in it.
5. Actually read what the AI wrote. This is the boring one, and also the most important one. You don’t need to understand every line, but you should at least skim the parts that touch user data, payments, or authentication. Ten seconds of reading catches problems that would otherwise take days to clean up.
None of this requires you to be an expert. It just requires you to stay in the loop instead of stepping out of it completely.
Why This Matters for Your Career, Not Just Your Code
Here’s the shift happening right now that most students haven’t fully clocked yet. A year after coining “vibe coding,” Karpathy himself said the term is outdated. His replacement term is “agentic engineering” — where you’re not just prompting AI and accepting whatever comes back, you’re actively directing it, reviewing it, and owning the outcome, the way a manager owns their team’s output.
That single shift is quietly becoming a hiring filter. Companies aren’t just looking for people who can get AI to generate code anymore — that part is becoming table stakes. They’re looking for people who can catch what the AI got wrong. That’s a very different, and much more valuable, skill.
If you’re a second or third-year student right now, this is genuinely good news. Experience used to mean “how many years have you spent writing code.” Increasingly, it means “how well do you understand what’s happening under the hood,” and that’s something you can start building today, regardless of how many years you’ve coded.
A small starting habit: the next time you vibe code something, before you call it done, spend five minutes trying to break your own app. Look at it the way someone trying to misuse it would. That single habit will teach you more about responsible AI use than any lecture will.
Closing Thought
Vibe coding isn’t going away, and honestly, it shouldn’t. It’s one of the most democratizing things to happen to software in years. But there’s a difference between using AI as a tool and handing it the keys and walking away.
The fairness, accountability, and transparency we talk about in responsible AI classrooms aren’t abstract ideas meant for policy papers. They show up in very real, very practical ways — in a misconfigured database, in a rate limit that was never set, in a line of code nobody bothered to read. The good news is that fixing this doesn’t take a computer science PhD. It just takes staying a little more awake at the wheel.
AI can write the code. Whether that code is safe, fair, and accountable — that part is still very much on us.
